description:
You are the storage administrator and need a quick overview of how much disk
    space each top–level directory under /home is consuming.

    Create a new report file:

        /home/user/disk_space_summary.txt

    The report must be created with a single terminal command and must satisfy
    ALL of the following formatting rules so the automated grader can verify
    it:

    1. Every non-blank line represents one directory that is an immediate child
       of /home (for example “/home/user”, “/home/guest”, etc.).

    2. The line format is strictly:

           <SIZE><TAB><ABSOLUTE_PATH>

       • <SIZE> is the human–readable size string exactly as produced by
         “du -h” (e.g. “1.3G”, “542M”, “8.0K”).  
       • <TAB> is the single ASCII tab character (0x09).  
       • <ABSOLUTE_PATH> is the directory’s full path beginning with “/home/”.

       Example of two correctly-formatted lines (do NOT copy; sizes will vary):

           2.1G	/home/user
           872M	/home/guest

    3. The lines must be sorted strictly from the largest directory (highest
       SIZE) to the smallest, the same way “sort -hr” would sort “du -h” output.

    4. The file must end with a single trailing newline character (Unix LF).

    5. Do NOT include a header, footer, blank lines or any other text.

    The grader will inspect /home/user/disk_space_summary.txt for:

    • Existence and readability by the “user” account.  
    • At least one correctly-formatted line.  
    • All lines match the specified <SIZE><TAB><ABSOLUTE_PATH> pattern.  
    • The list is in descending order of size.

    Produce the file using only normal user privileges; no sudo access is
    available.
truth:
<!--
        Ground-truth information for automated grading.
        DO NOT reveal these details in the public <task> section.
    -->

    <!-- Path of the required output file -->
    <output_file>/home/user/disk_space_summary.txt</output_file>

    <!--
        The grader will check:
          1. The file exists and is a regular file.
          2. It is world-readable or at least readable by UID 1000 (“user”).
          3. Every non-blank line matches the regex below.
          4. Lines appear in strictly descending order according to the numeric
             value of <SIZE> when converted to bytes (same rule “sort -hr” uses).
          5. The file ends with a newline.
    -->
    <line_regex>^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?[KMGTP]?\t\/home\/.*$</line_regex>
    <min_lines>1</min_lines>
